main_summary.summary: John Churchville, interviewed in Philadelphia on July 15, 2011 by Joe Mosnier for the Civil Rights History Project, recounts a trajectory from North Philadelphia sight-saving classes at T.M. Pierce School to SNCC voter-registration work in Southwest Georgia and Mississippi, and then back to Philadelphia organizing. Churchville joined the Ahmadiyya Muslim Community at 17 and later studied under Malcolm X at Temple Number 7 in Harlem. In 1962-63 he worked under Charles Sherrod in the Albany Project, registering voters in Lee County and Americus, where he brokered peace between two black teenage gangs. He taught literacy at a COFO Freedom School in Greenwood, Mississippi in 1964. Returning to Philadelphia, he founded the Northern Student Movement Freedom Library at Ridge and Jefferson in 1964 on a Dolfinger-McMahon Foundation grant, then converted it into the Freedom Library Day School, teaching preschoolers using the J.B. Lippincott Reading Series for fourteen years. He helped found the Black People's Unity Movement in February 1966, survived an August 13, 1966 Frank Rizzo-led police raid tied to the SNCC dynamite case, and later co-founded the Council of Independent Black Institutions (CIBI). Trained at Lutheran Theological Seminary at Philadelphia and Union Institute & University, he now leads the Liberation Fellowship Church of Jesus and its CDC, and the Greater Germantown Business Association, working to redevelop the Germantown Town Hall.
key_themes: SNCC voter registration in Southwest Georgia
Black nationalism and the Nation of Islam
Independent black education (Freedom Library Day School, CIBI)
Black People's Unity Movement and Philadelphia Black Power organizing
main_summary.historical_significance: Churchville's testimony documents the Philadelphia node of the post-1964 Black Power infrastructure, bridging Southern voter-registration fieldwork under Charles Sherrod, Northern student-movement community organizing, and the Nation of Islam's Atlanta and Philadelphia mosques under Minister Jeremiah Pugh. The account of the August 13, 1966 police raid on the Freedom Library, ordered under then-Police Commissioner Frank Rizzo, supplies a first-person record of the SNCC dynamite case and the surveillance environment surrounding BPUM. His description of the J.B. Lippincott Reading Series and the individual-prescriptive teaching method at Freedom Library Day School, and his role in CIBI's teacher-training institute, provide grounded primary-source material for the independent-black-institution movement of the 1970s. The interview also preserves direct recollections of Malcolm X at Temple Number 7, Diane Nash, Bernice Johnson Reagon, Faith Holsaert, Bob Mants, John Lewis, Dorie Ann Ladner, and Father Paul M. Washington.

chapters.title: North Philadelphia Childhood and Sight-Saving School
chapters.summary: Churchville describes growing up in North Philadelphia with extreme myopia, attending the sight-saving program at T.M. Pierce School, and confronting discrimination against sight-savers within the school's segregated structure. He recounts being elected student-council president in fifth grade and a seventh-grade bullying incident that nearly led him to use a zip gun.
related_events: Sight-saver discrimination at T.M. Pierce School
Adam Clayton Powell Sr High School enrollment

chapters.title: Mother Bertha and Musical Foundations
chapters.summary: Churchville credits his mother Bertha Churchville, choir director of the Village Concert Choir in North Philadelphia, as his closest influence. He describes her teaching of music, her openness to his exploration of Islam, and his own Simon Gratz High School choir direction under Joyce Smith that led to a music scholarship at Temple University.
related_events: Simon Gratz High School choir concerts

chapters.title: New York, Ahmadiyya Islam, and Malcolm X
chapters.summary: Churchville leaves Temple and moves to the Lower East Side at 19, encountering Marxists, poets, and black nationalists. He recounts converting to the Ahmadiyya Muslim Community at 17 under the name Yahya Abdul Hamid, then meeting Malcolm X at Temple Number 7 in Harlem and being struck by Malcolm's statement that 'my life is irrelevant.'
related_events: Move to East Village New York
Encounter with Malcolm X at Temple Number 7

chapters.title: SNCC and the Albany Project
chapters.summary: While working as a janitor at Columbia Presbyterian Medical Center, Churchville rides a bus to Georgia and joins SNCC under Reverend Charles Sherrod in the Albany Project. Assigned to Lee County and Americus, he requests a black partner to register young black voters, brokering a peace treaty between two warring teenage gangs in Americus and recruiting them to register voters.

chapters.title: Mississippi Freedom School and Southwest Georgia Violence
chapters.summary: Churchville teaches literacy at a COFO Freedom School in Greenwood, Mississippi. He recounts the Southwest Georgia terror, FBI complicity with local sheriffs, and the case of a Lee County mother whose son the sheriff threatened to lynch after a bakery driver raped the boy's sister. He praises Diane Nash, Bernice Johnson Reagon, Faith Holsaert, Bob Mants, Dorie Ann Ladner, and John Lewis.

chapters.title: Nation of Islam in Atlanta and Philadelphia
chapters.summary: Churchville joins the Nation of Islam mosque in Atlanta under Minister Jeremiah Pugh, organizing in Buttermilk Bottom and recruiting Atlanta University students. After Chicago headquarters under Elijah Muhammad rejects his economic-development proposal, Jeremiah is transferred to Philadelphia and Churchville returns with him, only to be denied the secretary position.
related_events: Atlanta NOI organizing in Buttermilk Bottom
Transfer of Jeremiah Pugh to Philadelphia

chapters.title: Freedom Library and the OAAU
chapters.summary: Churchville founds the Northern Student Movement Freedom Library at Ridge and Jefferson with a $1,200 Dolfinger-McMahon Foundation grant in 1964. After the riots that summer spare the storefront, he meets Malcolm X again and joins the Organization of Afro-American Unity. Minister Jeremiah Pugh warns his mother of the danger and orders the Fruit of Islam not to touch him.
related_events: Founding of Northern Student Movement Freedom Library

chapters.title: Freedom Library Day School and Independent Black Education
chapters.summary: Churchville transforms the library into Freedom Library Day School, teaching preschoolers phonics through the J.B. Lippincott Reading Series. He uses an individual-prescriptive method, has his mother join as a teacher, and operates the school for fourteen years. He critiques Amiri Baraka and Maulana Karenga's cultural-nationalist schools for prioritizing rhetoric over skills.

chapters.title: Black People's Unity Movement and the Rizzo Raid
chapters.summary: Churchville co-founds the Black People's Unity Movement in February 1966 with Father Paul M. Washington's church, Arthur Hall, John E. Allen Jr., and David P. Richardson Jr. He recounts a police informant, Casimiro Pereira, attempting to bring M16 rifles to the school, and the August 13, 1966 police raid led by then-Commissioner Frank Rizzo connected to the SNCC dynamite case. Attorney William Lee Akers tells him the warrant was valid.
related_events: Rizzo raid on Freedom Library August 13 1966

chapters.title: CIBI and National Black Education Work
chapters.summary: Churchville helps found the Council of Independent Black Institutions (CIBI) in the early 1970s, leading a two-week teacher-training institute at Freedom Library Day School. He frames his approach as revolutionary nationalism against the cultural nationalism of Amiri Baraka and Maulana Karenga, and recounts work with Chicago teachers and the post-CBS documentary speaking circuit.

chapters.title: Law Practice, Seminary, and Liberation Fellowship
chapters.summary: Churchville practices law as a farm-worker attorney in Cumberland County NJ then in Philadelphia, surviving a 1995 car accident that redirects him toward theology. He earns degrees at Lutheran Theological Seminary at Philadelphia and Union Institute & University, taught at LTSP for one year, and in 2005 founded the Liberation Fellowship Church of Jesus with his wife Nancy and a small congregation.

chapters.title: Germantown CDC and Town Hall Redevelopment
chapters.summary: Churchville describes the Liberation Fellowship CDC's HIV/AIDS testing program in Vernon Park, the founding of the Greater Germantown Business Association, and the $35 million redevelopment plan for Germantown Town Hall as a green building housing STEM education, green-trades training, and a business incubator across the street from Germantown High School.
related_events: Founding of Greater Germantown Business Association
